s: The Himalayas were formed about 50 million years ago when the Indian tectonic plate collided with the Eurasian plate.
q: The Himalayas were formed by the collision of which two tectonic plates?
e: This collision is still happening, pushing the Himalayas upward by about 5 mm per year. The range contains all fourteen peaks over 8,000 meters.

s: The capital of Honduras is Tegucigalpa.
q: What is the capital of Honduras?
e: Tegucigalpa serves as the vibrant capital of Honduras in North America!

s: Iceland sits directly on the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, making it one of the few places where a tectonic plate boundary is visible above sea level.
q: Iceland sits on which geological feature?
e: The island is being slowly pulled apart by the North American and Eurasian plates diverging at about 2.5 cm per year, creating dramatic rift valleys.

s: Iceland sits on the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, directly on top of the boundary between the North American and Eurasian tectonic plates.
q: Iceland sits on the boundary between which two tectonic plates?
e: Iceland is one of the few places where the Mid-Atlantic Ridge is visible above sea level. The plates are pulling apart at about 2.5 cm per year, creating volcanic activity.

s: India is located in the Northern and Eastern Hemispheres.
q: In which hemispheres is India located?
e: Based on its coordinates (28.6353°, 77.225°), India sits in the Northern and Eastern Hemispheres. Its capital New Delhi anchors this lower middle income nation in the South Asia region.

s: India surpassed China as the world's most populous country in 2023, with a population exceeding 1.4 billion.
q: Which country became the world's most populous in 2023?
e: India's population growth rate has been higher than China's due to China's former one-child policy. India is expected to continue growing while China's population has started to decline.

s: Indonesia is the world's largest archipelago, made up of over 17,000 islands — but about 6,000 of them are uninhabited.
q: How many islands make up Indonesia, the world's largest archipelago?
e: Stretching 5,120 km from east to west, Indonesia spans three time zones and sits on the Pacific Ring of Fire. Of its 17,508 islands, only about 6,000 are named and many are tiny volcanic or coral formations.

s: Indonesia is the world's largest archipelago, consisting of over 17,000 islands spread across 5,000 km of ocean.
q: How many islands make up Indonesia?
e: Only about 6,000 of Indonesia's islands are inhabited. The country straddles the equator and spans three time zones.

s: The International Date Line runs roughly along the 180° meridian in the Pacific Ocean, where crossing it changes the calendar date by one day.
q: Along which meridian does the International Date Line roughly run?
e: The line zigzags to avoid splitting countries into different dates. Traveling westward across it gains a day; traveling eastward loses a day.

s: The capital of Iran is Tehran.
q: What is the capital of Iran?
e: Tehran governs the vast territory of Iran, spanning 1.65M km²!

s: The capital of Iraq is Baghdad.
q: What is the capital of Iraq?
e: Baghdad serves as the vibrant capital of Iraq in Asia!

s: Iraq is located in the Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan region.
q: In which World Bank region is Iraq located?
a: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan
e: Iraq, with its capital at Baghdad, is part of the World Bank's Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan classification. The country has a population of approximately 46.0 million and is in the Northern and Eastern Hemispheres.

s: Ireland has no native snakes — and it's not because of St. Patrick. The island was too cold for reptiles during the last Ice Age, and snakes never recolonized after the ice melted.
q: Why does Ireland have no native snakes?
a: Snakes couldn't reach the island after the Ice Age ended
d: St. Patrick drove them out with magic
The climate is too cold year-round for snakes
Snakes are allergic to Irish peat
Ireland was never connected to any mainland
The Irish Sea creates a barrier snakes cannot cross
Snakes were hunted to extinction by ancient Celts
e: After the glaciers retreated about 10,000 years ago, the land bridge connecting Ireland to Britain disappeared before snakes could cross. Britain has three native snake species, but Ireland has none.

s: The capital of Jamaica is Kingston.
q: What is the capital of Jamaica?
e: Kingston is the tropical capital of the island nation of Jamaica!

s: Japan has a population of approximately 124.0 million.
q: What is the approximate population of Japan?
e: With around 124.0 million people, Japan is a high income country in the East Asia & Pacific region. Its capital city is Tokyo, located at coordinates 35.67°N, 139.77°E.

s: The capital of Jordan is Amman.
q: What is the capital of Jordan?
e: Amman serves as the vibrant capital of Jordan in Asia!

s: Jordan is located in the Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan region.
q: In which World Bank region is Jordan located?
a: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan
e: Jordan, with its capital at Amman, is part of the World Bank's Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan classification. The country has a population of approximately 11.6 million and is in the Northern and Eastern Hemispheres.

s: K2, at 8,611 meters, is the second-highest mountain in the world and is considered more dangerous to climb than Everest.
q: Which mountain is the second-highest in the world?
e: K2 sits on the China-Pakistan border in the Karakoram range. Its steeper slopes and unpredictable weather give it a much higher fatality rate than Everest.

s: Kaieteur Falls in Guyana has a single drop of 226 meters — about five times the height of Niagara Falls — yet it remains one of the most powerful and least-visited waterfalls in the world.
q: Which remote Guyanese waterfall has a single drop of 226 meters — five times Niagara's height?
e: Located in the rainforests of the Potaro-Siparuni region, Kaieteur Falls combines exceptional height with enormous volume. Despite being one of the world's most spectacular waterfalls, its remoteness means only a few thousand tourists visit per year.

s: The Great Migration in Kenya and Tanzania involves over 1.5 million wildebeest traveling in a continuous loop of about 1,800 miles, following the rains for fresh grass.
q: How many wildebeest participate in the Great Migration?
e: This is the largest overland migration on Earth. The wildebeest are joined by hundreds of thousands of zebras and gazelles. River crossings through crocodile-infested waters are among nature's most dramatic spectacles.

s: Mount Kilimanjaro in Tanzania is the highest free-standing mountain in the world at 5,895 meters.
q: What is the highest free-standing mountain in the world?
e: Unlike most tall mountains that are part of ranges, Kilimanjaro rises alone from the African plains. It is actually a dormant volcano with three volcanic cones.

s: Kiribati is the first country in the world to ring in each New Year, as parts of the nation use UTC+14, the world's earliest time zone.
q: Which country is the first to experience each New Year?
e: In 1995, Kiribati shifted parts of its territory across the International Date Line so the entire country would be on the same calendar day, creating the UTC+14 zone.

s: The Köppen climate classification system divides Earth into five main climate groups: Tropical, Dry, Temperate, Continental, and Polar.
q: How many main climate groups does the Köppen classification system have?
e: Developed by Wladimir Köppen in 1884, this system uses temperature and precipitation patterns to classify climates and is still the most widely used system today.

s: The capital of Kuwait is Kuwait City.
q: What is the capital of Kuwait?
e: Kuwait City serves as the vibrant capital of Kuwait in Asia!

s: The capital of Kyrgyzstan is Bishkek.
q: What is the capital of Kyrgyzstan?
e: Bishkek serves as the vibrant capital of Kyrgyzstan in Asia!

s: La Rinconada in Peru is the highest permanent settlement in the world at 5,100 meters above sea level — higher than Everest Base Camp — where about 50,000 people mine for gold.
q: What is the highest permanently inhabited settlement in the world, at 5,100 m elevation?
e: Located in the Andes near a glacier, La Rinconada has no running water, sewage system, or sanitation services. Miners work under the cachorreo system — they work for the mine owner for 30 days without pay, then get one day to keep whatever ore they find.

s: Lake Baikal in Siberia contains about 20% of the world's unfrozen surface freshwater, making it the largest freshwater lake by volume.
q: Which lake contains about 20% of the world's unfrozen surface freshwater?
e: At 1,642 meters deep, Baikal is also the deepest lake on Earth and is estimated to be about 25 million years old, making it the oldest lake as well.

s: Lake Baikal in Siberia, Russia, is the deepest lake in the world at 1,642 meters and holds about 20% of the world's unfrozen fresh surface water.
q: What is the deepest lake in the world?
e: Lake Baikal is also the world's oldest lake at about 25 million years old. It is home to over 3,600 species, two-thirds of which are found nowhere else on Earth.

s: Lake Chad in central Africa has shrunk by about 90% since the 1960s, from 25,000 km² to roughly 2,500 km².
q: By what percentage has Lake Chad shrunk since the 1960s?
e: Overuse of water for irrigation, drought, and climate change have caused this dramatic shrinkage. About 30 million people in four countries depend on the lake for their livelihoods.

s: Lake Hillier on Middle Island in Western Australia is bubblegum pink — and unlike other 'pink lakes' that change color, Hillier stays pink year-round, even when water is scooped into a glass.
q: Which Australian lake is permanently bubblegum pink, even when water is put in a glass?
e: Scientists believe the pink color comes from a combination of Dunaliella salina algae and halophilic archaea bacteria in the extremely salty water. Despite its vivid appearance, the water is safe to swim in. The lake was first noted by explorer Matthew Flinders in 1802.

s: Lake Maracaibo in Venezuela experiences lightning storms nearly 300 nights per year — a phenomenon called Catatumbo lightning that produces roughly 1.2 million strikes annually.
q: Where does the Catatumbo lightning phenomenon occur, producing storms nearly 300 nights per year?
e: Where the Catatumbo River meets Lake Maracaibo, warm Caribbean air collides with cold Andean mountain winds, creating a near-perpetual lightning storm visible from 400 km away. It was historically used by sailors as a natural lighthouse.

s: Lake Natron in Tanzania is so alkaline (pH 10.5) that it can calcify animals that die in it, yet it is the primary breeding ground for 2.5 million lesser flamingos.
q: Which ultra-alkaline Tanzanian lake is the primary breeding ground for 2.5 million flamingos?
e: The lake's extreme alkalinity comes from volcanic sodium carbonate. While deadly to most animals, the caustic water creates a perfect moat protecting flamingo nesting islands from predators. Flamingos' tough skin and scales resist the corrosive water.

s: Lake Titicaca on the border of Peru and Bolivia is the highest navigable lake in the world at about 3,812 meters above sea level.
q: What is the highest navigable lake in the world?
e: Lake Titicaca covers about 8,372 km² and has a maximum depth of 281 meters. The Uros people have lived on floating reed islands on the lake for centuries.

s: Liechtenstein and Uzbekistan are the only two doubly landlocked countries — they are landlocked and surrounded entirely by other landlocked countries.
q: How many doubly landlocked countries exist in the world?
e: To reach the ocean from either country, you must cross through at least two other countries, making them the most isolated from the sea of any nations on Earth.

s: Uzbekistan is one of only two doubly landlocked countries in the world — you must cross two borders to reach any coastline. The other is Liechtenstein.
q: Which Central Asian country is one of only two doubly landlocked nations on Earth?
e: A doubly landlocked country is surrounded entirely by other landlocked countries. Uzbekistan is bordered by Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Afghanistan, and Turkmenistan — all landlocked. Liechtenstein is bordered by landlocked Austria and Switzerland.

s: The capital of Laos is Vientiane.
q: What is the capital of Laos?
e: Vientiane serves as the vibrant capital of Laos in Asia!

s: Latvia's Venta Rapid (Ventas Rumba) is the widest natural waterfall in Europe at about 249 meters across, though it is only about 2 meters tall.
q: Which European country has the continent's widest waterfall?
e: Located in Kuldiga, this unusually wide but short waterfall stretches across the Venta River. During spring floods, salmon leap up the falls, and locals have historically caught them mid-jump using baskets.

s: The capital of Lebanon is Beirut.
q: What is the capital of Lebanon?
e: Beirut serves as the vibrant capital of Lebanon in Asia!

s: Lesotho has a population of approximately 2.3 million.
q: What is the approximate population of Lesotho?
e: With around 2.3 million people, Lesotho is a lower middle income country in the Sub-Saharan Africa region. Its capital city is Maseru, located at coordinates -29.5208°N, 27.7167°E.

s: Lesotho is one of only three countries in the world that are entirely surrounded by a single other country — it is completely enclosed by South Africa.
q: Which country is entirely surrounded by South Africa?
e: The other two fully enclosed countries are Vatican City (within Italy) and San Marino (also within Italy). Lesotho's lowest point at 1,400 m makes it the country with the highest lowest point in the world.

s: Lesotho is the only country in the world that lies entirely above 1,000 meters in elevation, earning it the nickname 'Kingdom in the Sky.'
q: What is unique about Lesotho's elevation?
d: It lies entirely below sea level
It has the lowest capital in Africa
It is surrounded by three countries
All its major cities are above 3,000 meters
e: Lesotho is also completely surrounded by South Africa, making it one of only three countries that are enclaved within a single other country (along with Vatican City and San Marino).

s: Lesotho is the only country in the world where every point of its territory sits above 1,000 meters elevation — earning it the nickname 'Kingdom in the Sky.'
q: Which country is called the 'Kingdom in the Sky' because its entire territory sits above 1,000 m?
e: Completely surrounded by South Africa, this small mountainous kingdom has its lowest point at 1,400 m, higher than the highest points of many countries. The Thabana Ntlenyana peak reaches 3,482 m, the highest point in southern Africa.

s: Libya holds the modern verified record for the highest air temperature ever reliably recorded in Africa: 55°C (131°F) measured in Aziziya in 1922, though this record was later invalidated, giving the title to Death Valley.
q: Which location holds the current official world record for highest air temperature?
e: The original Aziziya record of 58°C was discredited in 2012 by the World Meteorological Organization due to measurement errors. Death Valley's 56.7°C (134°F) from 1913 is now the official world record, though some scientists question that too.

s: Liechtenstein is one of only two doubly landlocked countries in the world — every country that borders it is also landlocked.
q: How many doubly landlocked countries exist in the world?
e: Liechtenstein is bordered by Austria and Switzerland, neither of which has ocean access. The only other doubly landlocked country is Uzbekistan, surrounded by five landlocked neighbors.

s: The border between Canada and the United States is the longest international border in the world at about 8,891 km.
q: What is the longest international border in the world?
e: This includes the border with Alaska. The US-Canada border is also known for being largely undefended, with many sections marked by only a cleared strip of forest.

s: Machu Picchu, the 15th-century Incan citadel, sits at about 2,430 meters above sea level in the Peruvian Andes.
q: At what approximate elevation does Machu Picchu sit?
e: Built under Inca emperor Pachacuti, Machu Picchu was abandoned during the Spanish conquest and remained unknown to outsiders until American historian Hiram Bingham publicized it in 1911.

s: Madagascar is the world's fourth-largest island, and about 90% of its wildlife is found nowhere else on Earth.
q: What percentage of Madagascar's wildlife is endemic (found nowhere else)?
e: Madagascar split from the Indian subcontinent about 88 million years ago, allowing its plants and animals to evolve in isolation, producing unique species like lemurs.

s: Malawi is located in the Southern and Eastern Hemispheres.
q: In which hemispheres is Malawi located?
e: Based on its coordinates (-13.9899°, 33.7703°), Malawi sits in the Southern and Eastern Hemispheres. Its capital Lilongwe anchors this low income nation in the Sub-Saharan Africa region.

s: The capital of Malaysia is Kuala Lumpur.
q: What is the capital of Malaysia?
e: Kuala Lumpur serves as the vibrant capital of Malaysia in Asia!

s: The capital of Maldives is Malé.
q: What is the capital of Maldives?
e: Malé serves as the vibrant capital of Maldives in Asia!
